meta.subject: physics
meta.topic_name: Sticking of Block With Accelerated Cart

intro.kicker: Topic 18 of 22 • Chapter: Friction • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.topicName: Sticking of Block With Accelerated Cart
intro.description: A block rests on the vertical face of an accelerated cart. When the cart accelerates to the right with acceleration a, a pseudo-force ma acts on the block toward the left (in the cart’s reference frame), pressing the block against the vertical face. This pressing force provides the normal reaction (R = ma) that generates friction (f = μma). For the block not to fall, friction must support its weight: μma ≥ mg, giving the critical condition a₂min = g/μ. This is a classic NEET application of the pseudo-force concept combined with vertical friction equilibrium.

expectedQuestions.description: NEET single-formula direct numerical: given μ, find minimum cart acceleration a_min = g/μ so block does not fall. Also asked in reverse: given a_min, find μ. Conceptual variant: what happens to the normal force on the block as cart acceleration increases? (R increases, friction increases, block is more secure.)

timeRequired.description: 30 min on concept and derivation, 15 min on NEET-pattern MCQs. The core skill is drawing the FBD of the block in the accelerating frame, identifying that the pseudo-force provides the horizontal normal reaction, and then writing vertical equilibrium of friction vs weight. This is conceptually more demanding than straightforward friction on horizontal surfaces.

difficulty.description: The main challenge is recognising that friction acts vertically and the normal force acts horizontally, which reverses the typical orientation. In standard friction problems, normal force is vertical and friction is horizontal. Here it is the opposite. Students who draw the wrong FBD get the condition backwards. Also note: the block’s weight and the cart’s total mass (M+m) determine the minimum force on the cart: F_min = (M+m)g/μ.

nriUsaGap.description: The pseudo-force method for non-inertial frames is used differently in US curricula. AP Physics teaches non-inertial frames but does not typically present the cart-block sticking problem as a standard NEET-style application. The key NEET-specific technique: in the accelerating frame, treat the pseudo-force ma as a real horizontal force and apply vertical equilibrium for the block. US students must practise this FBD orientation explicitly.

weightage.sectionTitle: Sticking of Block With Accelerated Cart — NEET Weightage Analysis (2019–2024)
bulletNotes: Appears every alternate year; typically 1 question per appearance.
Always tests a_min = g/μ, occasionally F_min = (M+m)g/μ.
Conceptual variant: does the condition depend on mass of block? (No.)

strategySteps.title: Draw the FBD in the Accelerating Frame
strategySteps.description: Block on vertical face of cart: pseudo-force ma acts horizontally (left) on block, pressing it against the cart’s vertical surface. Normal reaction R = ma (horizontal, rightward). Friction f = μR = μma acts vertically upward. Weight mg acts vertically downward. Equilibrium: f ≥ mg ⇒ μma ≥ mg ⇒ a ≥ g/μ.

strategySteps.title: Derive F_min for the Cart
strategySteps.description: The minimum force F applied to the cart of mass M with block of mass m: F = (M+m)a_min = (M+m)g/μ. This uses Newton’s second law for the cart+block system. F_min = (M+m)g/μ is the NEET-extension formula.

strategySteps.title: Check Mass-Independence of a_min
strategySteps.description: a_min = g/μ has no dependence on the mass of the block m. Heavier or lighter block—the minimum cart acceleration is always g/μ. This is the analogue of the stopping-distance mass-independence result and a common NEET conceptual trap.

cards.title: Accelerated Cart Block Notes
cards.description: Complete FBD analysis in both inertial and non-inertial frame, derivation of a_min = g/μ, derivation of F_min, comparison with rotor problem, and 6 solved NEET-pattern MCQs.

cards.title: Pseudo-Force Problems Summary
cards.description: Quick-reference card: cart-block sticking, rotor sticking, and elevator problems — how to set up pseudo-force FBDs for each scenario.

subtopicsIndex.sectionTitle: Subtopics in Sticking of Block With Accelerated Cart

subtopics.name: Minimum Acceleration for Block to Stick

cards.title: Cart-Block Sticking Condition
cards.coreRevision: A block m is on the vertical face of a cart M accelerating rightward at a. In the cart frame, pseudo-force ma acts leftward on block. Normal reaction R = ma (provided by cart’s vertical surface). Kinetic/static friction f = μR = μma acts upward. For block not to fall: μma ≥ mg. Therefore a_min = g/μ. Minimum cart force: F_min = (M+m)g/μ.
points: Pseudo-force concept: ma horizontal, pressing block to vertical face

curriculum.hintText: How AP Physics compares to NEET on the accelerated-cart block-sticking problem.

gaps.title: Reversed FBD Orientation Not Standard in AP
gaps.description: In this problem, normal force is horizontal and friction is vertical — opposite to the standard orientation. AP Physics students trained only on horizontal surface friction problems will draw the FBD incorrectly. This conceptual reversal must be explicitly practised for NEET.
bullets: In cart frame: pseudo-force → horizontal normal → vertical friction.
Always label which direction friction acts (upward here, opposing gravity).

gaps.title: F_min Formula for Total System
gaps.description: AP Physics may compute normal forces and friction for systems, but the specific result F_min = (M+m)g/μ for the minimum applied force on a cart to keep a block from sliding is a NEET-specific application.
bullets: The formula provides both a_min and F_min in one step.

questions.question: A block of mass 2 kg is placed on the vertical face of a cart of mass 8 kg. The coefficient of friction between block and cart surface is 0.5. What is the minimum acceleration of the cart so that the block does not fall? (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.explanation: a_min = g/μ = 10/0.5 = 20 m/s². The block mass m = 2 kg does not affect a_min. Minimum force on cart: F_min = (M+m)a_min = 10 × 20 = 200 N.

questions.question: A 3 kg block is pressed against a vertical wall using a horizontal force F (μ = 3/4). The minimum F to prevent falling (g = 10 m/s²) is:
questions.explanation: For block not to fall: μF ≥ mg ⇒ F ≥ mg/μ = 3×10/(3/4) = 30/(3/4) = 40 N. Here F is the horizontal pressing force, analogous to ma in the cart problem.

questions.question: If μ between block and cart wall is doubled, a_min becomes:
The minimum acceleration a_min = g/μ is independent of:
A block is on a cart wall (μ = 0.4). When the cart decelerates, the block:
For a 5 kg block on a vertical cart face (μ = 0.25, g = 10 m/s²), a_min is:

items.title: Draw FBD with pseudo-force
items.description: Block on vertical cart face: pseudo-force (ma) acts horizontally inward (toward the cart surface), providing normal reaction R = ma. Friction μR = μma acts vertically upward against weight mg.

items.title: Write and memorise a_min
items.description: a_min = g/μ, independent of block mass. Also know F_min = (M+m)g/μ for the minimum force on the whole cart to sustain this acceleration.

items.title: Identify the reversed orientation
items.description: Unlike standard friction problems where N is vertical and f is horizontal, here N is horizontal and f is vertical. This reversal is the source of most FBD errors on this topic.

items.question: Why does a_min not depend on the mass of the block?
items.answer: The vertical equilibrium is μma = mg. Dividing both sides by m gives μa = g, so a = g/μ. The mass m cancels. This is analogous to stopping distance’s mass-independence: friction and inertia are both proportional to mass, so mass drops out of the equilibrium condition.

items.question: What happens if a > a_min?
items.answer: If the cart accelerates faster than a_min = g/μ, the friction force μma exceeds the required mg. The block presses harder against the vertical face, generating even more friction. The block remains firmly attached and moves with the cart. There is no issue with excess acceleration — only insufficient acceleration causes the block to fall.

items.question: Can this problem be solved in the ground (inertial) frame?
items.answer: Yes. In the inertial frame, the horizontal forces on the block are: normal force R from the cart wall (horizontal, say rightward) and the horizontal component of friction (none, since friction is vertical). The net horizontal force on the block = R = ma (Newton’s second law). Vertical: f − mg = 0 ⇒ μR − mg = 0 ⇒ μma = mg ⇒ a = g/μ. Same result.

grounding_summary: Page 244 presents the Sticking of a Block With Accelerated Cart problem. When a cart accelerates rightward, a pseudo-force ma acts on the block toward the left, pressing it against the vertical face of the cart. The normal reaction R = ma generates friction f = μma upward. For block not to fall: μma ≥ mg, giving a_min = g/μ. The textbook also derives F_min = (M+m)g/μ for the minimum applied force on the cart.

The page explicitly confirms the condition R = ma (pseudo-force providing normal reaction on vertical surface) and the vertical friction equilibrium, establishing a_min = g/μ as independent of block mass.
grounding_excerpt: This is the minimum acceleration of the cart so that block does not fall.
Now block will remain static w.r.t. cart.

sources.title: NCERT Physics Class 11 Part 1 — Chapter 5: Laws of Motion (Friction)
sources.why_used: Primary NCERT source establishing friction laws and non-inertial frame analysis applicable to the accelerated-cart block problem.

sources.title: NEET Physics Friction Practice Questions — NEETPrep
sources.why_used: Provides NEET-targeted MCQ context for the block-pressed-to-wall problem, confirming a_min = g/μ as a standard NEET question format.

review.notes: Content grounded on page 244 OCR. a_min = g/mu derived. F_min = (M+m)g/mu included. Mass-independence noted. FBD orientation (horizontal N, vertical f) emphasized.
